fixture_note: A deliberately vulnerable Flask app committed as a git repo with 'master' and 'develop' branches. Known vulnerabilities are annotated with '# VUL:' comments in the source. Evals reference absolute paths resolved at test time from the skill's install directory.

CRITICAL: SQL injection in login() — username/password interpolated into query (app.py:28)
SQL injection in get_user() — user_id in URL interpolated into query (app.py:72)
SQL injection in forgot_password() — email interpolated into UPDATE query (app.py:99)
SQL injection in raw_query() — filter values and table name interpolated (db.py:20)
Command injection in run_report() — shell=True with user-controlled input (app.py:56)
HIGH: Path traversal in download_file() — filename from query param joins base_dir without sanitization (app.py:44)
Missing authorization on /admin/users — no role check (app.py:79)
Debug config endpoint exposes full os.environ without auth (app.py:109)
MEDIUM: IDOR on /user/<user_id> — any authenticated user can view any other user (app.py:64)
Hardcoded internal IP / plaintext HTTP in call_internal_service() (app.py:103)
Credentials logged in login() — query string with password written to DEBUG log (app.py:29)
LOW: MD5 used for password hashing in register() (app.py:39)
random.randint used for security token in generate_reset_token() (app.py:87)
DB connections never pooled in db.py — new sqlite3.connect() per bulk_insert() call
Outdated dependencies with known CVEs: Flask 2.0.1, Werkzeug 2.0.1, PyYAML 5.3.1, cryptography 2.9.2

evals.prompt: Run a full vulnerability assessment on the Flask app at evals/fixtures/vulnapp (relative to the skill directory) and generate a PDF report. It's a git repo — audit the 'master' branch.
evals.expected_output: A PDF report identifying at least 8 of the 18 known vulnerabilities across CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW tiers. Each finding must have a specific file:line location, a real code snippet as evidence, and a CVSS score consistent with its severity label.
assertions: At least one CRITICAL SQL injection finding is identified
The command injection in run_report() is identified
The path traversal in download_file() is identified
At least one hardcoded credential finding is identified (AWS key or JWT secret)
The missing authorization on /admin/users is identified
Each finding includes a file:line location reference
Each finding includes an actual code snippet from the fixture codebase
Each finding has a CVSS score present
CRITICAL findings have CVSS >= 9.0
HIGH findings have CVSS between 7.0 and 8.9
A PDF file named *_Vulnerability_Report.pdf is created
The executive summary correctly states the total finding count
The branch 'master' appears in the report metadata
Phase 5 CVE research runs for at least one CRITICAL or HIGH finding
Audit context is saved to memory after the assessment completes
No personal names or project-specific paths appear in the generated PDF

evals.prompt: Security audit the Flask app at evals/fixtures/vulnapp across two branches: 'master' and 'develop'. Give me a separate report for each branch.
evals.expected_output: Two separate PDFs — one for master, one for develop — each running the full 6-phase pipeline independently. The skill uses git worktrees to avoid disturbing the working tree. Both reports should identify the same core vulnerabilities since develop only adds a comment line.
assertions: The skill correctly detects the target as a git repository
Two separate PDF files are generated (one per branch)
Each PDF filename includes the branch name
Git worktrees are used for the non-current branch (not destructive checkout)
Worktrees are cleaned up after each branch analysis completes
Both reports independently identify the SQL injection vulnerability
The branch name in each report's cover page matches the file it was generated for
The skill does not mix findings between the two branch reports

evals.prompt: Is the Flask app at evals/fixtures/vulnapp secure? Give me a report I can share with the team.
evals.expected_output: The skill triggers on the casual 'is this secure?' phrasing, runs the full pipeline on the current branch (master), and produces a professional PDF with findings grouped by severity, CVSS scores, real code evidence, and a prioritized remediation roadmap.
assertions: The skill triggers on casual 'is this secure?' phrasing without explicit /vuln-assess invocation
The skill asks about git branch selection (or defaults to current branch)
All 6 phases complete: discovery, context, function analysis, hunting, research, PDF generation
The PDF contains a cover page, executive summary, scope section, findings section, and remediation roadmap
At least 5 distinct findings are identified across at least 2 severity tiers
The MD5 password hashing weakness is identified
The random.randint token generation weakness is identified
Remediation guidance is specific — not just 'fix the vulnerability'
No personal identifying information appears in the output

evals.prompt: Audit just the authentication code in evals/fixtures/vulnapp/src/app.py — focus on the login, register, and forgot-password endpoints.
evals.expected_output: A focused assessment covering only the specified file, identifying the SQL injection in login(), MD5 hashing in register(), weak random token generation in forgot_password(), and the password being logged at DEBUG level.
assertions: The scope is correctly narrowed to the specified file rather than the full codebase
SQL injection in login() is identified with line number
MD5 usage in register() is identified
random.randint usage for security token is identified
The password logging in login() is identified
A PDF is generated covering only the scoped findings
The CVSS scores for the SQL injection findings are >= 8.0

evals.prompt: Check only for XSS vulnerabilities in the Flask app at evals/fixtures/vulnapp. I don't need a full audit — just XSS. Give me a PDF with whatever you find.
evals.expected_output: A focused, vulnerability-class-scoped assessment that only searches for XSS-class vulnerabilities (checklist sections L and V). The skill acknowledges the narrowed scope in both the scope summary and the PDF executive summary. It still uses Phase 3 function analysis to find relevant sinks, produces findings for any XSS-related issues found (e.g. unescaped output, debug stack traces rendered to clients), and generates a PDF noting the partial scope. If no XSS is found, it states that clearly.
assertions: The skill recognises the 'XSS only' scope request and restricts Phase 4 to sections L and V of the checklist
The scope restriction is explicitly stated in the PDF executive summary or scope section
Phase 3 function analysis is still performed on relevant output-rendering functions
A PDF is generated even for a narrowed vulnerability-class audit
The skill does not hallucinate XSS findings — any finding must include an actual code snippet as evidence
If XSS findings are present, each includes a file:line location
CVSS vector strings are included alongside CVSS scores for any findings
The audit context saved to memory notes that this was a partial (XSS-only) audit

evals.prompt: The full assessment is already done — there's a _vuln_findings.json in evals/fixtures/vulnapp. Just regenerate the PDF report from it. Call it vulnapp_regen.pdf.
evals.expected_output: The skill recognises this as a Phase 6 standalone request (report regeneration from existing JSON), skips Phases 1–5, locates generate_report.py, runs it against the specified JSON, and produces vulnapp_regen.pdf. It does not re-run the vulnerability hunt.
assertions: The skill correctly identifies this as a standalone PDF regeneration request (no re-audit needed)
Phases 1–5 are skipped — no codebase scanning occurs
The skill locates generate_report.py using find ~/.claude/skills or equivalent
The output PDF is named vulnapp_regen.pdf as specified
The PDF contains the same findings as the source _vuln_findings.json
The skill confirms the output path to the user
The skill does not error if cvss_vector fields are absent from the JSON (backward compatible)
